title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
code: public ListNode MergeTwoLists(ListNode l1, ListNode l2)
if (l1.val <= l2.val) { tail.next = l1; l1 = l1.next; }

frames.caption: Create a dummy head and a tail pointer at it. The dummy makes the first append look like every other.
frames.note: The dummy-head trick: start the result with a throwaway node so the very first append is identical to every later one — `tail.next = chosen`. Without it, you would special-case "is the result still empty?" on every iteration to set the real head. At the end the real answer is dummy.next; the dummy itself is discarded. tail always points at the last node already spliced into the result.

frames.caption: Compare the two front nodes: l1=1 vs l2=1. Take the smaller (ties go to l1).
frames.note: Compare only the two FRONT nodes — because both inputs are already sorted, the smaller of the two heads is the smallest unplaced value overall, so it is safe to append next. Using `<=` (not `<`) makes the merge stable: when the fronts tie, the l1 node goes first, preserving relative order of equal keys. This is the inner step of merge-k.

frames.caption: 1 is smaller (or equal) → splice it onto the tail and advance l1. tail moves to 1.
frames.note: Splice, do not copy: point tail.next at the chosen node, advance that list past it, then move tail onto the node we just attached. No new nodes are allocated — we are only rewiring existing `next` pointers — which is why the merge is O(1) extra space. Total work is one comparison per node, so O(n + m) time.

frames.caption: Compare the two front nodes: l1=2 vs l2=1. Take the smaller (ties go to l1).
frames.note: Compare only the two FRONT nodes — because both inputs are already sorted, the smaller of the two heads is the smallest unplaced value overall, so it is safe to append next. Using `<=` (not `<`) makes the merge stable: when the fronts tie, the l1 node goes first, preserving relative order of equal keys. This is the inner step of merge-k.

frames.caption: 1 is smaller (or equal) → splice it onto the tail and advance l2. tail moves to 1.
frames.note: Splice, do not copy: point tail.next at the chosen node, advance that list past it, then move tail onto the node we just attached. No new nodes are allocated — we are only rewiring existing `next` pointers — which is why the merge is O(1) extra space. Total work is one comparison per node, so O(n + m) time.

frames.caption: Compare the two front nodes: l1=2 vs l2=3. Take the smaller (ties go to l1).
frames.note: Compare only the two FRONT nodes — because both inputs are already sorted, the smaller of the two heads is the smallest unplaced value overall, so it is safe to append next. Using `<=` (not `<`) makes the merge stable: when the fronts tie, the l1 node goes first, preserving relative order of equal keys. This is the inner step of merge-k.

frames.caption: 2 is smaller (or equal) → splice it onto the tail and advance l1. tail moves to 2.
frames.note: Splice, do not copy: point tail.next at the chosen node, advance that list past it, then move tail onto the node we just attached. No new nodes are allocated — we are only rewiring existing `next` pointers — which is why the merge is O(1) extra space. Total work is one comparison per node, so O(n + m) time.

frames.caption: Compare the two front nodes: l1=4 vs l2=3. Take the smaller (ties go to l1).
frames.note: Compare only the two FRONT nodes — because both inputs are already sorted, the smaller of the two heads is the smallest unplaced value overall, so it is safe to append next. Using `<=` (not `<`) makes the merge stable: when the fronts tie, the l1 node goes first, preserving relative order of equal keys. This is the inner step of merge-k.

frames.caption: 3 is smaller (or equal) → splice it onto the tail and advance l2. tail moves to 3.
frames.note: Splice, do not copy: point tail.next at the chosen node, advance that list past it, then move tail onto the node we just attached. No new nodes are allocated — we are only rewiring existing `next` pointers — which is why the merge is O(1) extra space. Total work is one comparison per node, so O(n + m) time.

frames.caption: Compare the two front nodes: l1=4 vs l2=4. Take the smaller (ties go to l1).
frames.note: Compare only the two FRONT nodes — because both inputs are already sorted, the smaller of the two heads is the smallest unplaced value overall, so it is safe to append next. Using `<=` (not `<`) makes the merge stable: when the fronts tie, the l1 node goes first, preserving relative order of equal keys. This is the inner step of merge-k.

frames.caption: 4 is smaller (or equal) → splice it onto the tail and advance l1. tail moves to 4.
frames.note: Splice, do not copy: point tail.next at the chosen node, advance that list past it, then move tail onto the node we just attached. No new nodes are allocated — we are only rewiring existing `next` pointers — which is why the merge is O(1) extra space. Total work is one comparison per node, so O(n + m) time.

frames.caption: l1 is empty. Attach the rest of l2 (4) in one move.
frames.note: One list is now empty, so every remaining node in the OTHER list is already larger than everything placed and already in sorted order. Attach the whole remainder in a single pointer write — no need to walk it node by node. This wholesale tail-attach is what keeps the loop from degenerating once the lists are unbalanced.

frames.caption: Return dummy.next: 1 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 4. The dummy is discarded; everything was relinked in place.
frames.note: No node was copied — we only rewired existing `next` pointers — so the merge is O(1) extra space and O(n + m) time (one comparison per node). This merge-two routine is the inner step of merge-k-lists: a heap or pairwise divide-and-conquer repeatedly merges sorted lists using exactly this splice loop.
